topic: Berlin memories — the barbershop in Kreuzberg

turns.content: tell me something... do you know the neighbourhood Kreuzberg in Berlin

Yes, Kreuzberg is a well-known neighbourhood in Berlin. It's historically been one of the most diverse and culturally vibrant areas of the city — large Turkish community, lots of artists and students, great street food scene. It was right next to the Berlin Wall during the Cold War, which shaped its character a lot. What's your connection to it?

I lived there... a long time ago. in my 20s. five years in Berlin. I worked in a barbershop on Oranienstraße... Salon Fritz, it was called. a German name but the owner was Turkish, Kemal abi. he thought a German name would bring more German customers... genau, he was right about that

That's a smart move by Kemal abi — "Salon Fritz" has that classic German barbershop ring to it. Oranienstraße is right in the heart of Kreuzberg too, so you were in the thick of it.

Five years is a real stretch of time. You must have built a life there. What was it like working as a barber in Berlin compared to Turkey?

ah... different world. in Istanbul, the barbershop is a social place, you know. men come, they sit, they drink tea, they talk for an hour. the haircut is almost... secondary. but in Germany... the appointment is at 14:00, the man arrives at 13:55, he sits down, you cut, he pays, he leaves. alles in Ordnung... everything in order. no tea, no conversation, just the cut

That's such a vivid contrast. The Turkish barbershop as a social hub versus the German efficiency model — appointment in, appointment out. Both work, but they're completely different experiences.

Did you ever manage to bring some of that Turkish social atmosphere into Salon Fritz? Or did you have to adapt fully to the German style?

at first I adapted... of course. you are a guest in someone's country, you respect the way. but slowly, slowly... the regulars started to stay longer. I would offer tea — Kemal abi had a little çaydanlık in the back — and some of the German customers, they loved it. they would say 'Mehmet, noch einen Tee bitte'... one more tea please. wunderbar. by my third year, Salon Fritz was half Turkish barbershop, half German. the best of both worlds

That's a beautiful evolution — you basically created a cultural bridge one cup of tea at a time. German customers discovering the social side of barbering, asking for refills in German while sitting in a Turkish tea atmosphere. Kemal abi must have loved what you brought to the shop.

Do you ever think about what Kreuzberg is like now? It's changed a lot since then — massive gentrification.

I have heard... coffee shops that charge 8 euros for a coffee, art galleries everywhere. in my time it was rough, you know... real neighbourhood, real people. the döner was 2 marks. 2 marks! now probably 7 euros. Kemal abi's rent was nothing compared to what it would be today... he is gone now, passed away maybe 10 years ago. I do not know if the shop is still there

I'm sorry to hear about Kemal abi. It sounds like he was an important figure in your life during those years.

You're right about the gentrification — Kreuzberg has changed dramatically. Rents have skyrocketed, many of the old Turkish businesses have been priced out. A traditional barbershop on Oranienstraße today would be paying several thousand euros a month. The döner is indeed around 7-8 euros now.

It's a different place, but the memories you have of it — the real Kreuzberg — those are from a special era.

natürlich... of course. you know what I miss most... the snow. Istanbul gets cold but Berlin cold is something else. minus 10, minus 15... and you walk to the shop in the dark, your breath like smoke, and then you open the door and the warm air hits you and the smell of the shaving cream... and Kemal abi already there, already set up, already drinking his first tea. 'günaydın Mehmet, kalt draußen, ja?' ... good morning Mehmet, cold outside, yes. every morning the same. I loved that

That's a vivid memory — I can almost feel the cold outside and the warm shop air. Those small daily rituals, the same greeting every morning, the reliability of it. There's something deeply comforting about that kind of routine, especially in a foreign country far from home.

Kemal abi sounds like he was a good mentor and a good man. Those morning greetings clearly stayed with you.

he was... he was. 'the master's eye fattens the horse,' we say. meaning the one who watches closely, who cares, he makes things grow. Kemal abi watched everything. every cut, every customer. he taught me how to run a business, not just how to cut hair... I came back to Istanbul because of my family, my mother was not well. but a piece of me stayed in Kreuzberg. a piece of me is still there

That's a powerful proverb and a fitting tribute to him. Learning the business side from someone who cared that deeply — that's the kind of education you can't get from a book.

And it makes sense that a piece of you stayed. Five years in your 20s, in a foreign city, learning your craft under a man like that — those aren't years you leave behind. They become part of who you are. I'm glad you shared this. It sounds like Berlin shaped you as much as Istanbul did.

it did... it did. sometimes I catch myself saying 'genau' to a customer and they look at me funny. a Turkish barber in Kadıköy saying genau... hah. anyway. thank you for letting an old man tell his stories. I will go make tea now... Kemal abi style

Ha — genau is a great word, your customers should adopt it! Thank you for sharing those stories. Enjoy your tea, and here's to Kemal abi and Salon Fritz. Some shops close, but the good ones live on in the people who learned there.
